text: Look at the log it's actually writing: `tail /var/log/connd/connd.log`. It repeats 'cannot open connection N/25: Too many open files' -- that error name (EMFILE) means a file-descriptor limit, not a real networking or disk problem.

Find its PID (`pgrep -f connd`) and check the limit the kernel is actually enforcing on it: `cat /proc/<pid>/limits | grep 'open files'`. Compare that against how many fds it's holding: `ls /proc/<pid>/fd | wc -l`.

The limit is set from `/etc/connd/connd.conf` (`NOFILE_LIMIT`) when the service starts, via `ulimit -n`. A hardening pass lowered it far below what this service actually needs at steady state (25 connections plus its own stdio).

Raise it in the config, then restart the service so the new limit actually takes effect (an already-running process's ulimit doesn't change on its own): edit `NOFILE_LIMIT` in `/etc/connd/connd.conf` up to something like `256`, then `sudo svc-ctl restart`.
